statusLine: EU fuel stocks remain under strain with 20 of 27 countries showing critical shortages in at least one fuel type, though overall averages exceed mandatory minimums.
fullAnalysis:
Despite EU-wide averages comfortably above the 90-day mandatory minimum (petrol 113 days, diesel 102.2 days, jet fuel 96.4 days), the distribution reveals significant vulnerabilities across member states. Twenty countries face critical shortages in at least one fuel category, with Romania experiencing the most severe situation across all three fuel types (petrol 40.4 days, diesel 30.2 days, jet fuel 76.4 days).

Jet fuel presents the most widespread concern, with 16 of 27 countries in critical status and an EU average of just 96.4 days—barely above the mandatory threshold. This vulnerability is particularly notable given ongoing Middle East supply disruptions that have contributed to Brent crude rising 52.3% since late February to $110.37/barrel. Eastern European nations appear most exposed, with Poland, Romania, and Luxembourg showing multiple critical fuel categories.

The price impact is clearly visible across pump prices, with significant variation between member states. The Netherlands faces the highest petrol costs at €2.341/L, while Malta enjoys the lowest at €1.34/L—a €1.00/L spread that highlights uneven market pressures. Current elevated oil prices, if sustained, could further strain countries already operating with thin stock margins and potentially trigger additional strategic reserve releases.
keyPoints: 20 of 27 EU countries show critical shortages in at least one fuel type, despite overall EU averages exceeding 90-day minimums
Jet fuel stocks pose greatest risk with 16 countries in critical status and EU average just 96.4 days above mandatory threshold
Romania faces most severe situation with all three fuel types in critical status (petrol 40.4 days, diesel 30.2 days, jet fuel 76.4 days)
Brent crude has surged 52.3% since late February to $110.37/barrel, driving pump price variations of up to €1.00/L between member states
Eastern European countries including Poland, Romania, and Luxembourg show multiple critical fuel categories, indicating heightened vulnerability
